text: The surgeon performs a right lower lobectomy using a subxiphoid port, an anterior camera port, and two anterior utility ports. A bipolar Maryland dissector is used for atesiolysis, demonstrating ease of movement and wrist articulation. The surgeon switches between instruments, including a sucker and standard diathermy, while managing the incomplete fissure by taking the inferior pulmonary vein first.

The surgeon, positioned at the patient's bedside, uses a bipolar Maryland dissector to dissect around the anterior hilum, precisely following the tissue planes. Bipolar fenestrated forceps are employed for blunt dissection around the inferior pulmonary vein, preparing it for stapling.

The surgeon uses a Maryland Dissector to dissect the pleura over the anterior hilum, identifying the superior pulmonary vein of the pulmonary artery. Fenestrated forceps are then used to encircle the superior pulmonary vein, utilizing their articulated movement for precise dissection. The surgeon staples the area using a subxiphoid port. Lymph nodes behind the superior pulmonary vein are identified and gently dissected out using the bipolar Maryland dissector.

The surgeon dissects around the anterior segmental pulmonary artery branch using alternating Maryland dissectors and fenestrated forceps, creating a clear path for stapling. The Cavidian Tristapler with the Signia gun is introduced through the subxiphoid port to staple the artery. In the subsequent thymectomy case, dissection begins at the junction of the pericardium and fat, following the phrenic nerve upwards. The bipolar Maryland dissector and fenestrated forceps are used to retract and dissect, providing a clear view of the innominate vein, rhema vein, and SVC. The surgeon uses subtle wrist movements to dissect fat between the innominate and rhema veins.
